name: Phase 21 · Migrations & Evolution
short: Schema · datastore · service extraction · zero-downtime — the art of changing the engine while the plane is flying

detail.what: Migration is the discipline of evolving your live, running, revenue-generating system without downtime, data loss, or user disruption. It covers four types of change, in increasing order of difficulty and risk: (1) Schema evolution — changing database tables (adding columns, changing types, dropping fields) while the application is running. (2) Datastore migration — moving from one database technology to another (MySQL → PostgreSQL, PostgreSQL → Cassandra, MongoDB → DynamoDB) while the system serves live traffic. (3) Service extraction — pulling a bounded context out of a monolith into an independent service with its own database and deployment. (4) Large-scale backfills — populating a new column or table with data derived from existing data, across billions of rows, without degrading production performance. Each of these sounds conceptually simple but is operationally treacherous. A column rename that takes 30 seconds on your laptop locks a 1-billion-row production table for hours. A datastore migration that 'just' moves data to a new engine risks subtle data loss from encoding differences, timezone handling, or NULL semantics. Service extraction that 'just' moves code to a new repo splits one transaction boundary into two, introducing consistency bugs. The common thread: every migration must be reversible at every step, the system must remain fully operational throughout, and the migration must be invisible to users. This requires patterns (expand-contract, dual-write, strangler fig) that are well-understood but require discipline to execute.
detail.why: Every successful system needs to evolve. Requirements change (you need multi-tenancy, you need global distribution, you need stronger consistency). Technology improves (PostgreSQL 16 is faster than 12, ScyllaDB is cheaper per-node than Cassandra, gRPC is faster than REST for internal calls). Scale changes (what worked at 100GB is unacceptable at 10TB). The teams growing the codebase discover that early architectural decisions — made correctly for the constraints of that time — are now the bottleneck. Without migration capability, you're stuck with day-one decisions forever, accumulating technical debt that compounds like financial debt until the system is unmaintainable. The migration capability — the ability to change any part of the system while it's running — is the single most important long-term architectural capability. It's what separates systems that age gracefully (Shopify's monolith, evolving for 15+ years) from systems that must be rewritten (Twitter's Ruby monolith, abandoned after 5 years of pain). Building migration into your engineering culture means you're never trapped by a past decision. The most dangerous phrase in engineering is 'we can't change that — it would require a migration.' If migration is too hard, the system can't evolve, and the system dies.
detail.numbers: Timeline benchmarks: simple schema change (add nullable column) = minutes. Complex schema change (change column type, add NOT NULL constraint) = hours to days using online tools. Datastore migration (MySQL → PostgreSQL, same schema) = 2–6 months including dual-write verification. Datastore migration (relational → NoSQL, different data model) = 6–18 months. Service extraction from monolith = 3–12 months per service. Large-scale backfill of 1 billion rows = 4–48 hours depending on write capacity and replication headroom. Key tools: gh-ost (GitHub's online schema migration for MySQL — copies table in background, swaps when done, near-zero downtime), pt-online-schema-change (Percona's equivalent, battle-tested), pg_repack (PostgreSQL equivalent for table reorganization), Debezium (Change Data Capture — streams database changes to Kafka, enabling real-time replication between datastores), AWS DMS (managed database migration service — handles initial bulk load + ongoing replication for common migration paths). Cost of NOT migrating: teams that avoid necessary migrations lose 20–40% of engineering velocity to workarounds for outdated architecture. A 6-month migration that's been deferred for 2 years has cost the equivalent of 2 years × 30% velocity loss = 7.2 engineer-months of productivity — far more than the migration itself would have cost.

tradeoffs.left: Big-bang cutover: fast (one weekend downtime), simple (switch everything at once), but high risk — if anything goes wrong, you're restoring from backup while your customers can't use the product
tradeoffs.right: Expand-contract / dual-write: slow (weeks to months of parallel operation), complex (maintain both old and new simultaneously), but safe — you can stop and roll back at any step without user impact

tradeoffs.left: Dual-write: application writes to both old and new datastore simultaneously — complex code, subtle consistency bugs between stores, but enables comparison and gradual cutover with zero-downtime rollback
tradeoffs.right: Hard cutover: clean, simple code (just switch the connection string), but if the new datastore has bugs, data corruption, or performance issues, you're rolling back under pressure with potential data loss

tradeoffs.left: Aggressive backfill (use all available write capacity): finishes faster, but degrades production performance and causes replication lag — acceptable during maintenance windows, dangerous during peak hours
tradeoffs.right: Throttled backfill (use <30% of write capacity): minimal production impact, but takes 3–10× longer — safer for 24/7 systems, frustrating for teams waiting on the migration to finish

pitfalls.name: Big-bang database rewrite
pitfalls.desc: 'We'll build the new system over 6 months, migrate the data in one weekend, and switch over Monday morning.' This plan fails 50%+ of the time (Joel Spolsky's 'Things You Should Never Do, Part I' is the canonical essay on this). The new system invariably has bugs, performance differences, and missing edge cases that were handled by 5 years of production patches in the old system. The migration weekend discovers data inconsistencies, encoding issues, and missing foreign keys. Monday morning, users find bugs. Tuesday, you're rolling back. Use strangler fig instead: migrate one endpoint, one table, one feature at a time. Each piece is validated in production before the next. It takes longer but it actually works.

pitfalls.name: Dual-write without comparison
pitfalls.desc: You're writing to both old and new datastores. You assume they're consistent. They're not. Subtle differences: timezone handling (MySQL TIMESTAMP vs PostgreSQL TIMESTAMPTZ), NULL semantics (some ORMs treat empty string and NULL differently), encoding (UTF-8 vs Latin-1 for special characters), auto-increment behavior, decimal precision. Without continuous comparison (shadow reads that verify both stores return the same data), you'll discover inconsistencies weeks after cutover when a customer reports wrong data. Always run shadow reads: for every read, query both stores, compare results, log discrepancies, and alert if the discrepancy rate exceeds 0.01%.

pitfalls.name: Forgetting about the long tail of queries
pitfalls.desc: You migrate the main CRUD operations to the new datastore. Everything looks great. Then you discover 47 'special' queries that analytics, admin tools, report generators, and batch jobs use — all hardcoded to the old schema. These break silently. Before starting any migration: inventory every query against the old system. Use pg_stat_statements (Postgres) or query_log (MySQL) to find every query pattern. Every one must be migrated.

pitfalls.name: Schema migration without feature flag
pitfalls.desc: You deploy a schema change (add column, change type) and the new code that uses it in the same release. If the code deploy is 5 minutes ahead of the schema migration, you get errors. If the schema migration is ahead of the code, you have unused columns. Always: deploy schema first (additive only — add columns, add tables), then deploy code that uses it (behind a feature flag), then enable the flag, then clean up the old code/schema. Each step is independently deployable and reversible.

pitfalls.name: Backfill that saturates replication
pitfalls.desc: You run a backfill that writes to 1 billion rows. Each write replicates to 2 replicas. Your replication bandwidth is shared with normal production writes. The backfill consumes 80% of replication capacity; normal writes queue up; replication lag grows to hours; read replicas serve stale data; users see inconsistencies. Always throttle backfills to <30% of available write capacity. Measure replication lag continuously during backfill. Pause if lag exceeds 30 seconds.

examples.name: Stripe's migration framework — migrations as a core competency
examples.desc: Stripe has migrated their core payment data across datastores multiple times (including a famous MongoDB → MySQL migration over 18 months). They built a reusable migration framework with three phases: (1) Dual-write: application writes to both old and new, with the old as authoritative. (2) Shadow-read: for every production read, also read from the new store and compare results — discrepancies are logged and investigated. (3) Gradual cutover: switch reads to the new store for increasing percentages of traffic (1% → 10% → 50% → 100%), monitoring for errors. This framework has been reused for many subsequent migrations, making each one faster and safer. The key insight: migration capability is itself an asset worth investing in. The framework cost months to build but saved years across subsequent migrations.

examples.name: Discord Cassandra → ScyllaDB — migrating trillions of messages
examples.desc: Discord stored trillions of chat messages in Cassandra. As their scale grew, they hit Cassandra's JVM garbage collection pauses (causing latency spikes) and high per-node costs. They migrated to ScyllaDB (Cassandra-compatible, written in C++, 3–10× better per-node performance). The migration: (1) Built a custom data migrator that read from Cassandra and wrote to ScyllaDB in parallel. (2) Ran dual-reads for weeks, comparing results to verify data integrity. (3) Gradually shifted read traffic from Cassandra to ScyllaDB (by percentage). (4) Cut over writes last. The entire migration took months but was invisible to users. The result: 3× better per-node performance and significantly lower latency due to ScyllaDB's lack of GC pauses.

examples.name: GitHub's gh-ost — online schema migration for MySQL
examples.desc: GitHub needed to alter tables with hundreds of millions of rows on their MySQL databases without locking the table (which would cause minutes of downtime). They built gh-ost (GitHub Online Schema Transmogrifier): it creates a 'ghost' copy of the table with the new schema, streams changes from the binary log to keep it synchronized, and performs an atomic swap when the copy is up-to-date. The swap takes <1 second. gh-ost is now the industry standard for MySQL online DDL. The lesson: invest in migration tooling early — it pays dividends on every future schema change.

examples.name: Shopify's monolith modularization
examples.desc: Rather than extracting microservices (which they deemed too costly operationally at their engineering team size), Shopify modularized their massive Rails monolith into ~100 'components' with enforced boundaries. This was itself a migration — identifying bounded contexts in a 5-million-line codebase, drawing boundaries, enforcing them with architecture tests (using Packwerk, a Ruby tool they built), and gradually untangling cross-boundary dependencies. The 'strangler fig' approach applied not to extracting services, but to modularizing a monolith — demonstrating that the pattern applies to any gradual architectural evolution.

levelUp.trigger: First migration that needs >5 minutes of downtime, or first ALTER TABLE that locks the table for >30 seconds — you've outgrown the luxury of 'just take it down for a minute'
levelUp.action: Adopt the expand-contract pattern for all schema changes. The pattern has 6 phases, each deployed independently: (1) Expand: add the new column/table (always additive — never rename or remove in the same step). Deploy this alone. (2) Dual-write: update application code to write to both old and new locations. Deploy this. (3) Backfill: run a background job that copies data from old to new for all existing rows. Make this idempotent (safe to re-run: use upserts or check-before-write). Throttle to <30% of write capacity. (4) Switch reads: update application code to read from the new location. Deploy behind a feature flag — toggle 1% → 10% → 100% of traffic. (5) Stop old writes: remove the dual-write code, write only to new. Deploy. (6) Contract: drop the old column/table. Deploy. Each phase is independently deployable, testable, and reversible. If anything goes wrong at step 4, flip the flag back; at step 3, re-run the backfill. Install online migration tools now: gh-ost for MySQL (handles ALTER TABLE without locking — watches the binlog and applies changes to a shadow table, then performs an atomic swap), pg_repack for PostgreSQL (reclaims bloated tables without locking), and Flyway or Alembic for version-controlled migrations (every schema change is a numbered, reversible script committed to version control alongside the code).

levelUp.trigger: First datastore migration (e.g., switching from MySQL to PostgreSQL, or from PostgreSQL to Cassandra for a specific workload) or first table exceeding 1 billion rows — the point where 'just ALTER TABLE' is physically impossible without downtime
levelUp.action: Build dual-write infrastructure — this is a significant engineering investment (4–8 weeks for 2 engineers) but enables safe datastore migration. Architecture: (1) Write proxy: a thin layer that intercepts writes and sends them to both old and new datastores. Use Debezium (open source CDC tool that captures database changes via the transaction log and streams them to Kafka — no application changes required for the old system) for a log-based approach, or application-level dual-write if you need transformation. (2) Shadow reader: a comparison service that, for every production read, also reads from the new datastore, compares the results, and logs discrepancies. Track discrepancy rate — target <0.001% before cutover. (3) Traffic shifter: a configuration (feature flag or percentage-based routing) that gradually shifts read traffic from old to new: 0% → 1% → 10% → 50% → 100%. Monitor latency, error rate, and data correctness at each step. Hold at each percentage for at least 1 week before advancing. (4) Rollback plan: at any step, you can shift reads back to 100% old. Writes continue to go to both datastores throughout. Only after reads are at 100% new for 2+ weeks do you stop writing to the old datastore. Technologies: Debezium (open source CDC, supports MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB, SQL Server, Oracle — streams changes to Kafka with exactly-once semantics), AWS DMS (managed migration service for common migration paths — handles initial bulk load + ongoing replication, easier to set up than Debezium but less flexible), custom dual-write library (gives you the most control but requires careful handling of failure modes: what happens if the new datastore write fails but the old succeeds?).

levelUp.trigger: Datastore migrations happening regularly (every 6–12 months as requirements evolve) or zero-downtime is a hard requirement for every migration — migration can't be a heroic project; it must be a routine operation
levelUp.action: Build a migration platform as a self-service internal capability. The platform provides: (1) Declarative migration definitions: engineer specifies source store, destination store, data transformation, and verification criteria. The platform handles the dual-write/shadow-read/gradual-cutover lifecycle automatically. (2) Automated verification: the platform continuously compares old and new stores, reports discrepancy rates, and blocks cutover until discrepancy rate falls below threshold. (3) Backfill-as-a-service: the platform manages throttled backfills on dedicated infrastructure (separate from production) with replication lag monitoring and automatic pause/resume. (4) Per-tenant cutover: for multi-tenant systems, migrate one tenant at a time — each tenant acts as its own canary. If a tenant's migration causes issues, roll back that tenant only. (5) Metrics and observability: real-time dashboard showing migration progress (rows migrated, discrepancy rate, old vs new latency comparison, estimated time to completion). Technologies: LinkedIn Brooklin (open source data streaming for datastore migrations — supports Kafka, MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle), Airbnb SpinalTap (open source CDC for MySQL with automatic schema evolution handling), Vitess online schema changes (built-in for MySQL/Vitess users — handles schema changes across sharded databases), CockroachDB online schema changes (built-in, handles ADD COLUMN, ADD INDEX, etc. without locking — if you're already on CockroachDB). For the platform itself: build on top of Debezium + Kafka + a custom orchestration layer using Temporal (workflow orchestration that handles retries, pauses, and resumption — ideal for long-running migration workflows that span days/weeks).

small.range: <1M rows, <10 tables, single database, deploying a few times per week
small.rec: Direct migrations are fine at this scale — the overhead of expand-contract isn't worth it yet when your largest table has 100K rows and ALTER TABLE takes <1 second. Use your ORM's migration framework: Rails migrations (built into ActiveRecord, reversible by default, excellent tooling), Alembic (Python/SQLAlchemy, version-controlled, auto-generates migration scripts from model changes), Flyway (Java ecosystem, SQL-based, simple and reliable), Prisma Migrate (TypeScript/JavaScript, generates SQL from schema declarations, excellent developer experience). Three rules even at small scale: (1) Never rename a column (it breaks all running code simultaneously) — instead, add the new column, dual-write, backfill, switch reads, drop old. (2) Always make changes additive in the same release (add columns, add tables — never remove in the same deploy as the code change). (3) Run migrations before deploying the new code, not simultaneously, so the schema is ready when the new code starts. Version-control every migration and never edit a migration that's been applied to production.

medium.range: 1M–1B rows, 10–100 tables, may have read replicas, deploying daily
medium.rec: Online migration tools become essential once tables exceed ~10M rows, because ALTER TABLE on a 10M-row table can lock for 30+ seconds — unacceptable during business hours. Use gh-ost (MySQL — creates a shadow copy of the table, replays binlog events to it, swaps atomically; takes minutes to hours but locks for <1 second) or pg_repack/pg_squeeze (PostgreSQL — reorganizes tables without exclusive locks). Always use expand-contract for any change that could break running code: column type change, column drop, table rename. Implement migration CI: a linter that checks every migration script for dangerous patterns (no `ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN ... NOT NULL` without a default, no `DROP COLUMN` without a preceding contraction phase, no `RENAME` — use Square's shift or similar linting tools). Test migrations against a production-sized database copy before running in production — a migration that takes 1 second on your dev database might take 1 hour on production. Technologies: gh-ost (GitHub, open source, MySQL — the gold standard for online MySQL DDL), pt-online-schema-change (Percona, MySQL — older but battle-tested alternative to gh-ost), pg_repack (PostgreSQL — reclaims bloated tables and can restructure without lock), Square's Shift (migration management UI for gh-ost/pt-osc — provides a web interface for reviewing, running, and monitoring migrations).

large.range: 1B+ rows, 100+ tables, sharded databases, multiple datastores, dozens of engineers making schema changes
large.rec: All migrations must follow expand-contract — no exceptions. Enforce with a schema linter in CI that rejects: column renames, column drops without a preceding contraction phase, NOT NULL additions without a default value, any DDL that could cause a lock. Automated backfill jobs: build a reusable backfill framework that reads source data in batches (1000–10000 rows at a time), applies the transformation, writes to the destination, and tracks progress (last-processed ID). The framework should: throttle based on replication lag (pause when lag >30s), be idempotent (safe to restart from any point), and report progress to a dashboard. For sharded databases: migrations must be rolled out per-shard, not globally, so a bad migration affects one shard (a fraction of users) before it reaches all shards. Technologies: Atlas (modern schema-as-code tool — you declare your desired schema, Atlas computes the migration diff and applies it safely, supports PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, and more — increasingly popular as a Flyway/Alembic replacement), Skeema (MySQL schema management, diffs desired vs actual schema and generates safe DDL), Liquibase (enterprise-grade, supports rollback, works across many databases, XML/YAML-based — more verbose than Flyway but more powerful). Custom backfill framework: build on top of your job queue (Sidekiq/Celery) with batch processing, progress tracking, and throttling.

hyper.range: Petabytes of data, thousands of tables across hundreds of shards, continuous schema evolution across thousands of engineers
hyper.rec: Migration is a platform service. Engineers declare the desired end state; the platform handles execution. Self-service platform capabilities: (1) Schema declaration: engineer writes the desired schema (using Atlas, Skeema, or custom DSL), the platform computes the migration plan, estimates duration, identifies risks, and shows the plan for review. (2) Automated execution: the platform applies the migration shard-by-shard, monitors replication lag and error rate, pauses on anomaly, and resumes automatically. No human babysitting. (3) Backfill on dedicated infrastructure: backfill jobs run on separate compute (not production) reading from replicas, writing to production primary — isolates backfill load from user traffic. (4) Per-shard rollout: migration applies to one shard first (canary), holds for a configurable period (e.g., 1 hour), then rolls out to remaining shards in waves. (5) Datastore migration-as-a-service: the platform manages dual-write, shadow-read, comparison, and gradual cutover for any supported datastore pair. Technologies: Vitess online schema changes (built-in for Vitess/MySQL — handles online DDL across thousands of shards with VReplication), CockroachDB online schema changes (built-in, handles schema changes in background without locking), custom migration platform (built on Temporal for workflow orchestration + Debezium for CDC + Kafka for event streaming + custom UI for visibility). Companies at this scale (Google, Facebook, Uber) all have custom migration platforms tailored to their specific datastore stack.

children.name: Schema Evolution
children.short: Expand-contract · online DDL · forward-only — change the schema without breaking the app

detail.what: Schema evolution is the practice of changing your database structure (adding columns, changing types, dropping tables, adding indexes) while the application is running, without downtime, data loss, or errors. The core challenge: your application code and your database schema are deployed at different times. If you change both simultaneously, there's a window where the old code runs against the new schema (or vice versa) — and if they're incompatible, you get errors. The expand-contract pattern solves this by ensuring the schema is always compatible with both old and new code at every step. It has 6 phases, each deployed independently: (1) Expand: add the new column/table (additive only — the old code ignores new columns it doesn't know about). (2) Dual-write: update code to write to both old and new locations. (3) Backfill: copy existing data from old to new. (4) Switch reads: update code to read from new location. (5) Stop old writes: remove dual-write, write only to new. (6) Contract: drop the old column/table. This looks like overkill for a simple column addition — and at small scale, it is. But at medium scale and above (tables with millions of rows, multiple services reading the same table), expand-contract is the only safe pattern. It's the difference between 'migration is a non-event' and 'migration caused an outage.'
detail.why: Database schema changes are the most common cause of deployment-related outages in production systems. The failure modes are specific and predictable: (1) Locking: ALTER TABLE on a large table acquires an exclusive lock, blocking all reads and writes for seconds to hours. (2) Incompatibility: new code expects a column that doesn't exist yet (code deployed before migration), or old code writes to a column that's been removed (migration applied before code). (3) Backfill race: new column has a NOT NULL constraint but existing rows have NULL — application crashes on read. (4) Replication lag: migration finishes on primary but replicas are behind — reads from replica see old schema. Each of these is preventable with the expand-contract pattern and online migration tools. The investment: learning the pattern (1 day), setting up tooling (1 day), discipline to follow it every time (ongoing). The payoff: never having a migration-related outage again.
detail.numbers: Expand-contract phases with timing: Phase 1 (Expand): add nullable column — instant for small tables, minutes for billion-row tables with online DDL tools. Phase 2 (Dual-write): code change — deploy in minutes. Phase 3 (Backfill): depends on table size and throttling. Formula: hours = total_rows / (write_rate_per_sec × replication_headroom). Where replication_headroom = (max_replication_writes_per_sec - normal_production_writes_per_sec). Typically 30–50% of max capacity. Example: 1B rows, write at 10K rows/sec with 50% headroom → 100K seconds → 27 hours. Phase 4 (Switch reads): code change — deploy in minutes. Phase 5 (Stop old writes): code change — deploy in minutes. Phase 6 (Contract): drop column — instant on PostgreSQL, may require table rebuild on MySQL. Total elapsed time: 2–5 days for a medium-sized migration, 1–2 weeks for a large one. Total human effort: 4–8 hours of actual work, spread across the phases.

tradeoffs.left: Full 6-phase expand-contract: safest, each phase independently reversible — but takes 3–5 deploys and 1–2 weeks elapsed time for what conceptually is a simple schema change
tradeoffs.right: Single-deploy migration: fast and simple — but if it fails, you're rolling back under pressure with the entire team watching and users complaining

small.range: Tables <1M rows, single database, few concurrent connections
small.rec: Direct migrations are fine. ALTER TABLE on a 100K-row table takes <1 second. Use your ORM's migration framework (Rails migrations, Alembic, Flyway, Prisma Migrate). Even at small scale, follow two rules: (1) Always add columns as nullable first, then backfill, then add NOT NULL constraint — never add a NOT NULL column without a default in a single step. (2) Never rename columns — add the new name, dual-write, backfill, switch reads, drop old. These habits cost nothing now and prevent outages later.

medium.range: Tables 1M–1B rows, may have read replicas, ALTER TABLE takes >10 seconds
medium.rec: Online migration tools are now essential. gh-ost (MySQL): creates a shadow copy of the table with the new schema, uses the binary log to copy changes in real-time, performs an atomic rename when the copy is synchronized — the lock is held for <1 second regardless of table size. pg_repack (PostgreSQL): similar approach for table reorganization. For column additions on PostgreSQL: `ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN` with a DEFAULT is instant in PostgreSQL 11+ (it stores the default in the catalog, not in each row) — one of PostgreSQL's best features. Always follow expand-contract for changes that affect running code (type changes, column drops, renames).

large.range: Tables >1B rows, sharded databases, dozens of teams making schema changes simultaneously
large.rec: Strict expand-contract enforced by CI. Schema linter checks every migration script: no renames, no drops without contraction, no NOT NULL without default, no full-table locks. Automated backfill framework: reads source in batches (configurable batch size, typically 1K–10K rows), applies transformation, writes to destination, tracks progress (stores last-processed primary key), throttles based on replication lag (pauses when lag >30s, resumes when <10s). All backfill jobs must be idempotent — safe to restart from any point without duplicating or corrupting data. For sharded databases: apply migrations shard-by-shard (canary shard first, then rolling wave) so a bad migration affects a fraction of users before reaching all.

hyper.range: Thousands of tables across hundreds of shards, continuous schema evolution
hyper.rec: Migration platform as self-service: engineer declares desired schema state, platform computes diff, generates safe migration plan, executes shard-by-shard with monitoring and automatic rollback. No manual DDL execution. Backfill on dedicated infrastructure to isolate from production load. Per-shard rollout with automatic canary hold. Tooling: Vitess online schema changes (handles ALTER TABLE across sharded MySQL via VReplication — shadow copy per shard, coordinated swap), CockroachDB online schema changes (native background schema change execution, no locking, automatic rollback on failure), or custom platform built on Temporal for orchestrating multi-phase migrations across hundreds of shards.

levelUp.trigger: First ALTER TABLE that takes >10 seconds (typically at ~5M rows), or first outage caused by a locking migration
levelUp.action: Install online migration tools: gh-ost for MySQL or pg_repack for PostgreSQL. These tools perform schema changes in the background without locking the table. Write a team guideline: 'every schema change follows expand-contract' with a checklist. Train the team on the 6-phase pattern — it takes 1 hour to learn and prevents a career-defining class of outages.

levelUp.trigger: Schema changes are becoming a source of incidents, or multiple teams are making conflicting schema changes to shared tables
levelUp.action: Implement CI enforcement: add a schema linter (custom or use Atlas's schema checking) that blocks merges containing dangerous patterns. Build a reusable backfill framework with throttling, idempotency, progress tracking, and replication lag monitoring. Migrate to schema-as-code (Atlas or Skeema) where the desired schema is declared in version control and migrations are auto-generated from diffs.

levelUp.trigger: Hundreds of schema changes per month across many teams, or sharded databases require coordinated cross-shard migrations
levelUp.action: Build a self-service migration platform. Engineers declare the desired schema; the platform computes the plan, estimates duration, identifies risks, executes shard-by-shard with monitoring, and rolls back automatically on anomaly. This requires 2–3 engineers and 3–6 months to build, but transforms schema evolution from a risky manual process to a routine self-service operation.

formulas.name: Backfill duration
formulas.note: replication_headroom_fraction = (max_replication_writes/sec - normal_production_writes/sec) / max_replication_writes/sec. Typically 0.3–0.5. A 1B row table backfilled at 10K rows/sec using 50% of headroom takes ~28 hours. Always run a small test (1% of data) first to validate timing estimates.

formulas.name: Migration risk score
formulas.note: Use this to prioritize: migrations with risk >1B should use online tools and expand-contract. Migrations with risk <1M can use direct ALTER TABLE.

pitfalls.name: NOT NULL on add without default
pitfalls.desc: Adding a NOT NULL column to a table with existing rows fails on most databases because existing rows would violate the constraint. On MySQL, ALTER TABLE rewrites the entire table to add the default, holding a lock for the duration. On PostgreSQL 11+, ADD COLUMN with DEFAULT is instant — but ADD COLUMN NOT NULL without DEFAULT still fails. Always: add nullable → backfill → alter to NOT NULL. Three deploys, zero risk.

pitfalls.name: Forgetting to backfill before reading
pitfalls.desc: New column 'status' added (nullable). New code deployed that reads 'status' and expects it to be one of ['active', 'inactive']. Existing rows have NULL for 'status'. Code crashes with NullPointerException/TypeError on every old row. Always backfill before deploying code that reads the new column. If backfill takes too long, make the code handle NULL as a valid value (treat as default).

pitfalls.name: Replication lag during backfill
pitfalls.desc: Backfill writes at full speed to the primary. Each write replicates to 2 replicas. Normal production writes continue. The combined write load exceeds replication bandwidth. Replication lag grows: 1 minute, 10 minutes, 1 hour. Read replicas now serve data that's an hour old. Users see stale data. Production SLOs are violated. Fix: always monitor replication lag during backfills. Throttle to <30% of write capacity. Pause automatically when lag exceeds 30 seconds. Resume when lag drops below 10 seconds.

pitfalls.name: Migration ordering across services
pitfalls.desc: Service A adds a column to a shared table. Service B adds a different column to the same table. Both migrations run simultaneously — one succeeds, one fails due to lock contention (or both create conflicting intermediate states). At scale with multiple teams: use a migration lock (only one migration per table at a time) or a migration queue. Atlas and Skeema handle this by computing diffs from the desired state rather than running manual migration scripts.

children.name: Datastore Migration
children.short: Dual-write · shadow-read · gradual cutover — the highest-stakes migration in engineering

detail.what: Datastore migration is moving from one database technology to another while the system serves live traffic: MySQL → PostgreSQL, PostgreSQL → Cassandra, MongoDB → DynamoDB, or any combination. This is the highest-stakes migration in engineering because: (1) data models may differ (relational → document, row-oriented → column-oriented), requiring transformation of every record; (2) query semantics differ (JOIN behavior, NULL handling, transaction isolation, timestamp precision), causing subtle correctness bugs; (3) performance characteristics differ, potentially invalidating all your capacity planning; and (4) data loss is irrecoverable — unlike code bugs which can be patched, lost or corrupted data may be gone forever. The standard playbook (pioneered and documented by Stripe) has 5 phases: (1) Backfill: copy all existing data from old to new, with transformation. (2) Dual-write: application writes to both old (authoritative) and new. (3) Shadow-read: for every production read, also read from new and compare — log discrepancies, don't serve the new result yet. (4) Cutover: gradually shift reads from old to new (1% → 10% → 100%). (5) Cleanup: stop writing to old, decommission. Each phase can take weeks. The total migration typically takes 6–18 months for a large datastore.
detail.why: You migrate datastores when the current one has become a bottleneck that can't be solved by optimization within the same technology. Common triggers: (1) Scale ceiling: PostgreSQL single-node can't handle your write throughput; you need Cassandra's horizontal scaling. (2) Cost: DynamoDB at high throughput is 10× more expensive than self-managed Cassandra. (3) Data model mismatch: you stored time-series data in PostgreSQL, but query patterns don't need JOINs and you need 100× write throughput — Cassandra fits naturally. (4) Feature needs: you need global distribution with tunable consistency — CockroachDB or Spanner. (5) Operational burden: self-managed MySQL is consuming 30% of your DBA team's time; managed Aurora reduces this to near zero. Whatever the trigger, the migration is a significant engineering investment. The decision to migrate should be backed by concrete numbers: 'we need 100K writes/sec, our current DB tops at 20K, and vertical scaling adds $50K/month for only 40K — migration to Cassandra gives us 100K+ at $20K/month.' Without concrete numbers, consider whether optimization of the current datastore (indexes, query rewriting, caching, read replicas) could solve the problem for 10× less effort.
detail.numbers: Timeline benchmarks: planning + proof-of-concept = 2–4 weeks. Backfill implementation + testing = 2–4 weeks. Backfill execution (depends on data volume): 100GB = days, 1TB = weeks, 10TB = months. Dual-write + shadow-read implementation = 2–4 weeks. Shadow-read comparison period = 2–4 weeks (minimum — you need enough traffic to exercise edge cases). Gradual cutover = 2–4 weeks (hold at each percentage level for at least a few days to observe all traffic patterns). Cleanup = 1–2 weeks. Total: 6–18 months for a large migration, 2–4 months for a small one. Key metric during shadow-read: discrepancy rate. Target <0.001% before cutover. Common discrepancy sources: timezone handling (store in UTC everywhere!), decimal precision (MySQL DECIMAL vs PostgreSQL NUMERIC), NULL vs empty string, character encoding (UTF-8 vs Latin-1 for accented characters), auto-increment behavior (don't rely on DB-specific ID generation in the new store).

tradeoffs.left: Fast cutover: less time running two datastores (which is expensive and complex), but less verification time means more risk of undiscovered discrepancies — a subtle data corruption bug might not appear until months later
tradeoffs.right: Slow, thorough cutover: weeks of shadow-read comparison covering all traffic patterns including rare edge cases, but you're paying for and operating two datastores in parallel the entire time — double the infrastructure cost and operational burden

tradeoffs.left: Application dual-write: your code explicitly writes to both stores — you control transformation logic, but it adds complexity to every write path and requires handling partial failures (what if the new store write fails?)
tradeoffs.right: CDC-based replication (Debezium): captures changes from the old store's transaction log and streams them to the new store — no application code changes for writes, but transformation is done in the CDC pipeline (potentially complex) and there's inherent lag (seconds to minutes)

medium.rec: Application-level dual-write with manual cutover. Architecture: (1) Add a feature flag that controls whether writes go to old-only, both, or new-only. (2) Behind the flag, write to both stores on every mutation. Handle failures: if the new store write fails, log the failure and continue (old store is authoritative). Run a reconciliation job hourly that catches and retries failed writes. (3) Backfill historical data: a batch job that reads from old, transforms, writes to new. Make it idempotent (check if record exists before writing). Throttle to avoid overloading the new store. (4) Shadow reads: for each production read, also read from new (asynchronously, not in the request path), compare, log discrepancies. Track discrepancy rate in a dashboard. (5) When discrepancy rate < 0.001% for 2+ weeks: shift reads gradually (1% → 10% → 100%) using the feature flag. Monitor latency and error rate at each step. (6) When reads are 100% new for 2+ weeks with no issues: stop writing to old. (7) Decommission old store. Technologies: Debezium (open source CDC — captures database changes via transaction log, streams to Kafka; supports MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB, SQL Server, Oracle — the standard tool for database replication without application changes), AWS DMS (managed migration service — handles initial bulk load + ongoing CDC for common migration paths like MySQL→PostgreSQL, Oracle→Aurora; simpler to set up than Debezium but less flexible for custom transformations), custom dual-write library (gives full control but requires careful handling of partial write failures).

large.rec: Automated migration framework with comprehensive comparison. Build (or adopt) a framework that manages the full lifecycle: backfill → dual-write → shadow-read → comparison dashboard → per-tenant/per-shard cutover → cleanup. The framework should support: per-tenant cutover (migrate one tenant at a time, each acting as a canary — if issues appear, roll back that tenant without affecting others), automated comparison (every shadow read is compared and discrepancies are categorized: data difference, timing difference, or format difference), and rollback at any point (switching a tenant back to old store requires one config change). Technologies: Stripe-style migration framework (if building custom — Stripe has documented their approach extensively in blog posts and conference talks), Debezium + Kafka + custom comparison service (the open-source stack: Debezium captures changes, Kafka buffers them, a custom consumer applies to the new store and compares), LinkedIn Brooklin (open source data streaming platform for moving data between stores — supports Kafka, MySQL, PostgreSQL, and more, designed for large-scale migrations).

hyper.range: Petabytes of data, continuous migration as a routine operation
hyper.rec: Migration is a platform capability. The platform manages: distributed CDC (Debezium at scale — hundreds of source tables captured simultaneously, each streaming to Kafka), parallel backfill on dedicated infrastructure (read from read replicas, write to new store, throttled by replication lag), automated 100% shadow-read comparison (every read to the new store is verified against the old — not sampled, full comparison), per-shard/per-tenant gradual cutover with automated rollback, and a migration dashboard showing progress, discrepancy rate, latency comparison, and estimated time to completion. The platform team handles the infrastructure; product teams initiate migrations through a self-service interface. Technologies: Airbnb SpinalTap (open source CDC for MySQL — captures changes with automatic schema evolution handling, designed for scale), LinkedIn Brooklin (data streaming for cross-store replication at massive scale), custom migration platform built on Temporal (for orchestrating the multi-week, multi-phase lifecycle of a migration with retries, pauses, and automatic recovery).

levelUp.trigger: Dual-write code scattered across many services, or manual comparison is missing edge cases — the migration process itself is becoming a reliability risk
levelUp.action: Build (or adopt) a reusable migration framework that encapsulates the dual-write/shadow-read/comparison/cutover lifecycle. Start with Debezium for CDC (eliminates application-level dual-write complexity — the old store's transaction log becomes the source of truth for replication). Build a comparison service that runs shadow reads and categorizes discrepancies. Add per-tenant cutover capability. This takes 4–8 weeks to build but makes every subsequent migration 5× faster and 10× safer.

levelUp.trigger: Multiple migrations happening simultaneously across different teams, or migration duration exceeds 12 months due to operational complexity
levelUp.action: Build a migration platform. Self-service interface where teams declare: source, destination, transformation rules, verification criteria. The platform handles: parallel backfill on dedicated infra, continuous CDC via Debezium at scale, full shadow-read comparison with categorized discrepancy reports, per-shard gradual cutover with automatic rollback, and a real-time progress dashboard. Invest 3–6 months with 3–5 engineers. The platform pays for itself across the organization's migration needs within 1–2 years.

examples.name: Stripe's MongoDB → MySQL ledger migration
examples.desc: Stripe migrated their core financial ledger from MongoDB to MySQL over 18 months. They built a custom dual-write framework that wrote every mutation to both stores, compared every read, and gradually shifted traffic. The framework was deliberately designed for reuse — Stripe has since used it for multiple subsequent migrations. Key insights from their public talks: (1) The comparison phase found dozens of subtle discrepancies (timezone handling, decimal precision) that would have caused real financial errors if not caught. (2) They held at each cutover percentage for 1+ weeks to cover all traffic patterns including month-end batch processing. (3) Total engineering cost: ~6 engineer-years. This sounds enormous, but the MongoDB limitations were costing them more in workarounds and operational overhead.

examples.name: Discord's Cassandra → ScyllaDB migration
examples.desc: Discord migrated trillions of chat messages from Cassandra to ScyllaDB (a C++ reimplementation of Cassandra with 3–10× better per-node performance and no JVM garbage collection pauses). Because ScyllaDB is Cassandra-compatible at the protocol level, the migration was simpler than a typical datastore migration: same data model, same query patterns, same drivers. They built a custom data migrator for the initial backfill, ran dual-reads for weeks to verify data integrity, and gradually shifted traffic. Post-migration: tail latency improved dramatically (ScyllaDB has no GC pauses) and they reduced their cluster size by 3×.

children.name: Strangler Fig
children.short: Incrementally replace a monolith — the safe alternative to the big rewrite

detail.what: The Strangler Fig pattern (named by Martin Fowler after the strangler fig tree that grows around and eventually replaces its host tree) is a strategy for migrating from a legacy system to a new one by gradually routing specific traffic to the new system while the old system continues serving everything else. Over time, more and more traffic routes to the new system until the old system handles nothing and can be removed. The key mechanism is a routing layer (reverse proxy, API gateway, or service mesh) that sits in front of both old and new systems and decides, per request, which backend handles it. This lets you: (1) migrate one endpoint at a time (start with the simplest, lowest-risk endpoint), (2) validate each migration in production with real traffic before moving to the next, (3) roll back any individual endpoint to the old system if issues arise, and (4) run old and new in parallel for comparison (dual-read/shadow traffic). The pattern is the opposite of a 'big-bang rewrite' where you build the entire new system, then switch all traffic at once. Big-bang rewrites have a >50% failure rate (Netscape, Borland, Twitter's initial Ruby rewrite) because the new system invariably has bugs, missing features, and performance issues that only appear under real production traffic. The strangler fig succeeds because each piece is validated in production before the next is attempted.
detail.why: Every large software system eventually reaches a point where the current architecture — chosen correctly for the constraints of 5 years ago — has become a bottleneck. The monolith that was perfect for 10 engineers is a merge-conflict nightmare for 100. The MySQL database that was ideal for 1TB is groaning under 50TB. The PHP codebase that launched the company is now impossible to hire for. The instinct is to rewrite from scratch: 'we'll build it right this time, in a modern language, with a clean architecture.' This instinct is almost always wrong. Joel Spolsky (Netscape), Twitter (initial Ruby → JVM attempt), and countless others have demonstrated that big-bang rewrites fail because: (1) the old system represents 5 years of production-learned edge cases, bug fixes, and optimizations that the rewrite team doesn't know about, (2) the rewrite takes 2× longer than estimated (always), during which the old system must also be maintained (feature-frozen products lose customers), and (3) the cutover day reveals hundreds of discrepancies that weren't caught in testing. The strangler fig avoids all of these failure modes: each piece is small enough to understand fully, production-validated before the next, and independently reversible. It takes longer (6 months to 3 years vs the rewrite team's estimate of 6 months) but it actually works. The most successful migrations in tech history (Twitter's eventual Ruby → Scala, Amazon's monolith → services, Shopify's modularization) all followed the strangler fig approach.
detail.numbers: Success rates: strangler fig migrations succeed ~90% of the time (each piece is small and independently validated). Big-bang rewrites succeed ~30–50% of the time (and 'success' often means 'shipped, but with a terrible experience for months after'). Timeline: strangler fig takes 6 months to 3 years depending on system size and team resources. Big-bang rewrites are estimated at 6 months but actually take 12–24 months. Cost comparison: strangler fig's total cost is typically lower because it doesn't require a separate rewrite team — the same engineers who maintain the old system migrate it piece by piece, learning the new system incrementally. Routing overhead: the proxy/gateway adds 1–3ms per request — negligible. Migration velocity: typically 1–3 endpoints per week once the team is practiced.

tradeoffs.left: Big-bang rewrite: faster if it works (all at once), but >50% failure rate — and when it fails, you've wasted months-to-years of engineering on a system you can't ship. The most expensive possible failure mode in software engineering.
tradeoffs.right: Strangler fig: slower (6 months to 3 years), requires maintaining two systems in parallel for the duration, but each step is validated and reversible. The outcome is predictable: it works, every time, it just takes patience.

tradeoffs.left: Rewrite: fresh codebase, clean architecture, no legacy decisions — the system you'd build if starting from scratch today. Feels great intellectually.
tradeoffs.right: Strangler: messy intermediate state where some requests go to old, some to new, data is in both places, and the routing layer has complex rules. Feels ugly but works.

medium.range: Reverse proxy routes by URL path
medium.rec: Put a reverse proxy (Nginx, Envoy, or your API gateway) in front of the old monolith. Configure routing: new endpoints (that you're building from scratch or rewriting) route to the new service; everything else routes to the old monolith. Start with the simplest, most self-contained endpoint — something with no complex state dependencies on the rest of the monolith (e.g., a health check, a public API, a simple CRUD endpoint). Validate it in production for 1–2 weeks. Then migrate the next endpoint. Gradually, the monolith handles less and less traffic until it can be decommissioned. Technologies: Nginx (simplest configuration — `location /api/v2/users { proxy_pass http://new-service; }` — free, battle-tested), Envoy (more powerful routing rules, supports header-based and percentage-based routing — essential for canary-style migration), Kong or AWS API Gateway (managed, GUI for routing rules, easier for non-infrastructure teams).

large.range: Feature flags + parallel implementation + data comparison
large.rec: At larger scale, simple URL-path routing isn't sufficient — you need to route by user, by tenant, or by percentage. Use feature flags (LaunchDarkly, Flagsmith, or custom) to control which users hit old vs new. Start migrating by user cohort: internal employees first (they'll report bugs fastest and most forgivingly), then beta users, then a small percentage of production users, then everyone. For endpoints that read/write data: run old and new in parallel (both receive the same requests, both write to their own data stores), compare responses, and alert on discrepancies. This 'parallel run' approach validates correctness before any traffic actually switches. Technologies: LaunchDarkly ($10/seat/month for pro — the industry standard for feature flags with targeting rules, percentage rollout, and automated rollback), Envoy traffic splitting (route 1% to new, 99% to old, with Envoy's weighted routing), Istio canary (Kubernetes service mesh with fine-grained traffic control — route by header, cookie, geography, or percentage).

hyper.range: Programmable mesh-driven migration with automated comparison
hyper.rec: At hyper-scale, the routing layer is a programmable service mesh (Istio, Envoy) that routes by arbitrary request properties: HTTP headers, cookies, geographic origin, user ID hash, tenant classification, feature flag state. The migration team writes routing policies declaratively (e.g., 'route 5% of requests from EU users for endpoints matching /api/v2/* to the new service'). An automated comparison system A/B-tests old vs new at the response level: both systems process the same request, responses are compared, discrepancies are categorized and reported. Auto-rollback: if the error rate or discrepancy rate for the new service exceeds a threshold (e.g., >0.1%), the routing policy automatically reverts to 100% old. Technologies: Istio traffic policies (declarative YAML-based routing rules applied to the mesh — the most powerful routing available), custom service mesh with programmable routing (large companies often build custom because they need routing rules that integrate with their feature flag system, tenant management, and migration tracking), feature flags at edge (Cloudflare Workers, Fastly Compute@Edge — evaluate routing rules at the CDN edge for sub-millisecond decision-making, before the request even reaches your infrastructure).

levelUp.trigger: Simple path-based routing is insufficient (you need per-user, per-tenant, or percentage-based routing), or the migration involves endpoints that share state with the rest of the monolith
levelUp.action: Implement feature-flag-based routing. Deploy LaunchDarkly or Flagsmith (open source alternative). Wrap each migrated endpoint in a flag: `if (flag.isEnabled('new-user-service', userId)) { routeToNew() } else { routeToOld() }`. Start with internal users, expand to 1% → 10% → 100%. For endpoints that share data with the monolith (e.g., the new service needs to read from the monolith's database during transition): either have the new service call the monolith's API for shared data (keeps the monolith as source of truth during transition), or replicate the shared data via CDC (Debezium) and accept eventual consistency during the migration period.

levelUp.trigger: Multiple strangler-fig migrations happening simultaneously across different teams, or the routing rules for old-vs-new are becoming unmanageably complex
levelUp.action: Deploy a programmable service mesh (Istio on Kubernetes) as the routing layer for all traffic. Migration routing policies are expressed as Istio VirtualService resources — version-controlled, reviewable, and auditable. Build an automated comparison system: for every request that routes to the new service, also shadow-route to the old service (asynchronously), compare responses, and report discrepancies. The comparison runs continuously during migration, catching regressions the moment they appear. This infrastructure serves all ongoing and future migrations, not just the current one.

examples.name: Twitter Ruby → Scala — the canonical strangler fig
examples.desc: Twitter's original Ruby on Rails monolith famously couldn't handle their traffic growth (the 'Fail Whale' era, 2007–2010). Rather than a big-bang rewrite, they gradually extracted services: first the messaging pipeline (Kestrel), then the timeline service, then the user service, then search, each rewritten in Scala/JVM. The migration took 5+ years. Some core Ruby code still exists today. Each extracted service was validated in production independently. The result: Twitter went from crashing daily to handling 500K+ tweets/minute with sub-second delivery. The lesson: strangler fig is slow, messy, and unglamorous — but it works, even at Twitter's scale.

examples.name: Shopify's monolith → modular monolith — strangler without extraction
examples.desc: Shopify applied the strangler fig pattern not to extract microservices, but to modularize their 5-million-line Rails monolith into ~100 'components' with enforced boundaries (using Packwerk, a tool they built for boundary enforcement). Each component is a bounded context (e.g., payments, shipping, catalog) with a clear public API. Cross-boundary calls go through defined interfaces. This is a 'strangler' applied to internal architecture — gradually replacing spaghetti code with modular code, one boundary at a time. The key insight: the strangler fig pattern isn't just for extracting services. It's a general strategy for any gradual architectural evolution. And modularization within a monolith is often more cost-effective than service extraction.

examples.name: Amazon's 2002 API Mandate — the original service extraction
examples.desc: In 2002, Jeff Bezos issued the famous 'API mandate': all teams must expose their functionality through service interfaces, all communication must happen through these interfaces, no direct access to another team's data store. This was the beginning of Amazon's transition from monolith to services — executed over many years, one service at a time, using what we would now call the strangler fig pattern. Each team extracted their functionality into a service, with the monolith gradually routing traffic to the new services. This migration took years and involved thousands of engineers, but it enabled Amazon to scale to the company it is today. The mandate is the most famous example of strangler-fig-at-organizational-scale.
